title: Swiss roll
subtitle: Jam and cream sponge rolled into a cylinder
excerpt: A thin, flexible sponge baked quickly at high heat, spread with jam and whipped cream, and rolled into a tight log while still warm. The technique is entirely in the rolling: a cold sponge cracks.
sourceNotes: Swiss roll (also called a jelly roll in North America) appears in British baking guides from the mid-19th century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, documents the rolled sponge. The technique of rolling the hot sponge around a sheet of baking paper to set the curve, then unrolling and filling, is documented in early 20th-century British household baking guides.

content.text: A Swiss roll is a one-technique bake: the sponge must be rolled while hot. Leave it ten minutes too long and it cracks on the first bend. The
technique is not optional; it is the whole skill of the bake.

attrs.prepNote: warmed slightly to make it spreadable

content.text: Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional. Line a 33×23 cm baking tray with baking paper, greasing it on both sides of the paper so the sponge releases easily. Whisk the eggs and sugar with a hand mixer for 5 minutes until very thick, pale, and tripled in volume. The mixture should leave a thick ribbon on the surface for 3 seconds when the whisk is lifted. Fold in the flour in two batches using a large metal spoon, cutting and turning rather than stirring. Pour into the prepared tin and tilt to spread to the corners. Bake for 8 to 10 minutes until the sponge is pale gold, just set on top, and springs back to a very light press.

While the sponge bakes, lay a clean sheet of baking paper on a work surface and dust it with caster sugar. Turn the hot sponge out directly onto the sugared paper immediately from the oven. Peel the lining paper off carefully. Using the sugared paper underneath, roll the sponge tightly from one short end into a log, rolling the paper inside it. Hold the roll for 30 seconds, then leave rolled and paper-wrapped to cool for 20 minutes.

Carefully unroll the sponge. Spread the warm jam evenly over the surface, leaving 1 cm clear at the far edge. Spread the whipped cream over the jam. Roll up again, this time without the paper inside, using the paper underneath to lift and guide the roll. Place seam-side down on a serving plate and dust with caster sugar.
